findings.title: Single-flight lock guards only one of two live bulk-publish routes
findings.why_it_matters: The new _bulk_publish_lock is presented (in code comments, the frontend API comment, and the double-submit test) as a hard no-double-scheduling guarantee, but it is a module-level lock scoped to a single Flask view function in webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py. The pre-existing, unconditionally-registered legacy route POST /ce:draft/bulk-publish-now (webui_app/routes/drafts.py:90-96) calls the exact same underlying DraftAPI.bulk_publish_now() against the same singleton _drafts_store (SQLite) and singleton _scheduler (APScheduler) via its own separate DraftAPI() instance, and never touches _bulk_publish_lock. Two near-simultaneous requests -- one to /api/v1/drafts/bulk-publish-now and one to /ce:draft/bulk-publish-now (e.g. an operator with the legacy Jinja page and the new SPA page open in two tabs, or any direct HTTP client) -- both proceed concurrently through the non-atomic get-item/update-item/add-job sequence, defeating the single-flight guarantee the plan explicitly designed and tested for. The interleaving can corrupt per-item rollback bookkeeping (see the duplicate-id finding) or leave the two batches' scheduler add_job(..., replace_existing=True) calls silently clobbering each other's run_date/state for overlapping ids.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the single-flight guard into DraftAPI.bulk_publish_now() itself (or a shared module both routes import) so every entry point -- new and legacy -- serializes on the same lock; alternatively, deprecate/redirect the legacy /ce:draft/bulk-publish-now route now that the SPA has parity, so only one live entry point remains.
evidence: webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py:29-35 -- _api = DraftAPI() and _bulk_publish_lock = threading.Lock() are both local to this module; the lock is acquired only inside drafts_bulk_publish_now() (line 146) in this same file.
webui_app/routes/drafts.py:14-15 -- _draft = DraftAPI() is a SEPARATE instance created in the legacy blueprint module.
webui_app/routes/drafts.py:90-96 -- ce_draft_bulk_publish_now() calls _draft.bulk_publish_now(ids) directly, with no lock, no 409 path, and no awareness of webui_app.api.v1.drafts._bulk_publish_lock.

webui_app/routes/__init__.py:26,58 -- drafts_bp (the legacy blueprint containing the unguarded route) is registered unconditionally, not gated behind BACKLINK_PUBLISHER_SPA, so the bypass route is live in every deployment mode including the default (SPA-on) mode.

DraftAPI has no instance state (no locks, no queues) of its own -- both DraftAPI() instances read/write the same module-level _drafts_store and _scheduler singletons imported from webui_app/scheduler.py, so two different facade instances still share the exact same race-prone mutable state.

tests/test_webui_api_v1_bulk_ops.py's test_webui_drafts_bulk_publish_now_double_submit_returns_409 only proves reentrancy protection via a synchronous NESTED call to the same endpoint from inside the mocked function body -- it never exercises a concurrent call to the sibling legacy route, so the bypass is untested.

findings.title: Duplicate ids in bulk-publish-now corrupt rollback, orphan a draft as scheduled
findings.why_it_matters: _require_ids() (webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py) does not deduplicate the ids array before handing it to DraftAPI.bulk_publish_now(), and that facade method captures each item's original state for rollback via a fresh _drafts_store.get_item(item_id) call INSIDE the per-id loop rather than from a single upfront snapshot. If the same id appears twice (e.g. [a,a,b], trivially producible by any raw HTTP client even though the SPA's own selection UI can't emit it) and a later id in the batch (b) throws during scheduling, the exception handler's rollback replays store_rollbacks in append order -- so the SECOND entry for a (captured AFTER the first iteration already mutated it to scheduled) overwrites the true original state. Concretely: the rollback's job-removal loop successfully removes the job APScheduler now holds for a, but the rollback's STORE loop re-applies the stale mid-batch scheduled snapshot as the restored state. The draft is left with status=scheduled and a stale scheduled_at in the past/near-future, but with NO backing scheduler job -- it will never fire and will show as permanently scheduled in the UI, even though the response claims all changes were rolled back.
findings.suggested_fix: Deduplicate ids in _require_ids() (or at the top of bulk_publish_now/bulk_cancel) before processing, and/or capture each item's original state once via a single upfront batch read rather than per-iteration get_item calls inside the mutate loop.
evidence: webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py:54-61 -- _require_ids() only checks isinstance(ids, list) and ids, then does [str(i) for i in ids] with no set()/dedup step.

webui_app/api/drafts_api.py:330-345 -- inside the for i, item_id in enumerate(ids) loop, item = _drafts_store.get_item(item_id) is re-fetched every iteration and store_rollbacks.append((item_id, original_status, original_scheduled)) captures whatever the store currently holds -- for a repeated id this is the state left by the PRIOR iteration's own update_item call two lines above, not the true pre-batch state.

webui_app/api/drafts_api.py:357-370 -- on exception, the rollback iterates completed_jobs (which for a repeated id contains the same job id twice) calling _scheduler.remove_job(job_id), then separately iterates store_rollbacks (which for a repeated id contains two DIFFERENT captured states) calling _drafts_store.update_item(item_id, status=status, scheduled_at=sched_at) -- the later, corrupted entry wins as the restored state.

_scheduler.add_job(..., id=item_id, replace_existing=True) at drafts_api.py:347-354 means a repeated id's second add_job call silently replaces (not duplicates) the first schedule, so even the happy-path (no failure) response overcounts completed_jobs in the batch-count message relative to the number of distinct drafts actually affected.

findings.title: Unbounded ids array holds bulk-publish lock for duration proportional to N
findings.why_it_matters: Neither _require_ids() nor DraftAPI.bulk_publish_now() caps the size of the ids array. A legitimate select-all action on a large draft queue (or a careless/scripted client) can pass thousands of ids; the entire per-id loop (one SQL SELECT, one SQL UPDATE, and one _scheduler.add_job() call per id) executes synchronously INSIDE the request handler while _bulk_publish_lock is held for the whole loop. Every other call to /api/v1/drafts/bulk-publish-now -- including a legitimate operator's accidental double-click, or a retry after a transient failure -- is rejected with 409 for the entire duration of that one oversized batch, with no way for the caller to know how long the in-progress state will last. This turns the new safety guard into a self-inflicted, input-size-controlled availability gap on the very feature it protects.
findings.suggested_fix: Cap ids length in _require_ids() (422 above a documented max, mirroring how other bulk endpoints in this codebase bound batch size), or move long-running batch scheduling off the request thread entirely (background job + poll, matching the keep_alive.py recheck pattern already in this codebase).
evidence: webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py:54-61 -- _require_ids() has no upper bound check on len(ids).

webui_app/api/drafts_api.py:320-379 -- bulk_publish_now() iterates every id synchronously (SQL get + SQL update + _scheduler.add_job), and this entire call happens inside the try/finally that holds webui_app/api/v1/drafts.py's _bulk_publish_lock (lines 146-155), so lock-hold duration scales linearly with len(ids) regardless of server threading model.

The sibling history/bulk-recheck and drafts/bulk-cancel endpoints share the same unbounded-ids pattern via the same _require_ids() helper, so the same class of problem is reachable from all three new endpoints, not just bulk-publish-now.

findings.title: Cross-endpoint race: bulk-cancel result can be reverted by a concurrent rollback
findings.why_it_matters: bulk_cancel() has no single-flight lock at all (unlike bulk_publish_now), and both bulk operations perform non-atomic read-then-write sequences against the same shared draft rows. If a bulk-cancel call and a bulk-publish-now call race on an overlapping draft id, and the publish-now batch later fails on a DIFFERENT id (triggering its rollback path), the rollback in bulk_publish_now restores its own PRE-RACE snapshot of the shared item -- overwriting the cancel's legitimate pending write with a stale scheduled state, while the rollback also removes the scheduler job it had itself just added. The net result: the draft is left showing status=scheduled in the store with NO backing scheduler job (it can never fire on its own), even though both concurrent requests reported success. _publish_draft_job's own status re-check at fire time (webui_app/scheduler.py:105) prevents the more severe phantom-publish-after-cancel variant of this race, but the state-integrity/false-success-message variant survives.
findings.suggested_fix: Route bulk-cancel through the same coordination primitive as bulk-publish-now (or a per-item lock keyed on draft id) so the two mutating operations cannot interleave on overlapping ids; alternatively make each facade method re-verify current store state immediately before its rollback/final write rather than trusting a snapshot captured earlier in the request.
evidence: webui_app/api/drafts_api.py:381-411 -- bulk_cancel() acquires no lock (module-level or otherwise) before mutating _drafts_store/_scheduler, unlike bulk_publish_now which is at least guarded (only against itself, and only via the new API route) by _bulk_publish_lock.

webui_app/api/drafts_api.py:366-370 -- the rollback loop in bulk_publish_now's except-block writes _drafts_store.update_item(item_id, status=status, scheduled_at=sched_at) from a snapshot captured before the batch started, with no re-check of whether another request (e.g. a concurrent bulk_cancel) has since legitimately mutated that same row.

webui_app/scheduler.py:102-106 -- _publish_draft_job re-checks item.get('status') != 'scheduled' at fire time, which mitigates the reverse (worse) ordering where a stray job would otherwise publish a draft the user believed was cancelled -- so this finding is scoped to the state-integrity/orphan-status outcome, not an unwanted publish.

residual_risks: The new _bulk_publish_lock is a plain threading.Lock(), which only coordinates threads within a single OS process. No Dockerfile/gunicorn/wsgi config in this worktree shows evidence of a multi-worker-process deployment today (Dockerfile CMD is python serve.py; webui.py's __main__ calls Werkzeug's app.run() directly, a single process) -- so this specific risk currently appears moot. However, this codebase has previously had to solve the identical class of problem with a stronger primitive: src/backlink_publisher/publishing/adapters/medium_auth.py explicitly notes its in-process threading.Lock() is only for gunicorn --threads safety and layers a separate cross-process fcntl.flock sidecar lock for the guarantee that actually needs to survive multiple workers. If this app's deployment model ever adds multi-process workers, the new _bulk_publish_lock's 409 guarantee would silently stop working with no test able to catch the regression.

DraftAPI.bulk_publish_now/bulk_cancel/bulk_delete all perform multi-step, non-transactional store+scheduler mutations (read state, mutate store, mutate scheduler) with no encompassing lock or DB transaction; every additional concurrent entry point added in the future (beyond the two identified here) inherits the same class of interleaving risk.
testing_gaps: No test exercises a concurrent call to the legacy /ce:draft/bulk-publish-now route while /api/v1/drafts/bulk-publish-now holds _bulk_publish_lock -- the only double-submit test simulates reentrancy on the SAME endpoint via a nested synchronous call, not a true race against the sibling unguarded route.

No test sends a duplicate id within a single ids array to /api/v1/drafts/bulk-publish-now or /api/v1/drafts/bulk-cancel, so the rollback-corruption and message-miscount behavior described above is unverified.
No test asserts an upper bound (or absence of one) on ids array length for any of the three new bulk endpoints.
No test interleaves bulk-cancel and bulk-publish-now on an overlapping draft id to verify the final store/scheduler state stays self-consistent.
